coredata.dc:title: Biomass-based carbon dots: Synthesis and application in imatinib determination
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zhengyu
Zhang, Zhengwei
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized by chemical oxidation treatment of the starch. The as-prepared CDs exhibit high fluorescence intensity, excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp. Based on fluorescence quenching, a novel method for the determination of imatinib was developed with CDs. The linear relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity and the concentration of imatinib (10–400μgmL−1) was obtained with a relation coefficient of 0.9977. The recovery was in the range of 98.0–102.0% with a relative standard deviation of 1.5%, and the limit of detection was 2.4×10−3 μgmL−1. The possible quenching mechanism between CDs and imatinib was found to be charge transfer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Characterization
Imatinib
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of imatinib with as-prepared CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs under varied excitation wavelengths, inset: optical images under UV light; (B) FT-IR spectrum of as-prepared CDs.

(A) The effect of different CDs concentration of on the fluorescence intensity and quenching rate (left to right: 10.13, 20.25, 30.38, 41.50, 50.63 and 60.75μgmL−1); (B) the effect of pH of PBS buffer solution on fluorescence quenching rate of the system; (C) the effect of reaction temperature on the fluorescence intensity of the system (from 15 to 60°C); (D) the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity of the system (within 1h).

The fluorescence spectra of CDs (41.50μgmL−1) in the presence of various concentrations of imatinib (from a to i, were 0.00, 10.01, 20.02, 40.04, 60.06, 80.08, 100.1, 200.2 and 400.4μgmL−1) in a pH 3.0 PBS solution. Inset: the standard curve of the concentration of imatinib versus F 0/F −1.

(A) Absorption spectra of imatinib (a) and fluorescence spectra of CDs (b); (B) absorption spectra of CDs (a), imatinib (b), CDs and imatinib (c) and superposition data of CDs and imatinib (d); (C) FT-IR spectra of CDs-imatinib (a) and imatinib (b); (D) schematic illustration for interaction mechanism between CDs and imatinib.
Test for the interference of coexisting substances.

head.ce:title: Biomass-based carbon dots: Synthesis and application in imatinib determination

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized by chemical oxidation treatment of the starch. The as-prepared CDs exhibit high fluorescence intensity, excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp. Based on fluorescence quenching, a novel method for the determination of imatinib was developed with CDs. The linear relationship between the change of fluorescence intensity and the concentration of imatinib (10–400μgmL−1) was obtained with a relation coefficient of 0.9977. The recovery was in the range of 98.0–102.0% with a relative standard deviation of 1.5%, and the limit of detection was 2.4×10−3 μgmL−1. The possible quenching mechanism between CDs and imatinib was found to be charge transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials have attracted great interest due to excellent optical properties as fluorescence probes [1,2]. To date, lots of nanomaterials (quantum dots, gold/silver nanoparticles, iron oxide nanoparticles, etc.) have been studied and utilized in the field of biomedicine and biotechnology [3,4]. Carbon dots (CDs) are novel and fascinating nanomaterials in the carbon nanomaterials family comprising of carbon nanotube, carbon nanohorn, graphite and nanodiamond [5,6]. CDs superior to conventional semiconductor quantum dots in terms of high fluorescent stability, environmentally friendly and biocompatibility have drawn much attention [7–9]. Thus, it is necessary to develop a facile and environmentally benign method for the CDs synthesis with low-cost raw materials.

The advantages of these inspiring features have encouraged the development of fluorescence probes. There are many reports on CDs as fluorescence probes for chemical substances, such as metal ions [10–12], small organic molecules [13–15], biomolecules [16,17], etc. Imatinib (Fig. 1 ) is a specific inhibitor of the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase, and has revolutionized the treatment of chronic myeloid leukemia (CML) and gastro-intestinal stromal tumor (GIST) [18,19]. Thus, the high-performance and accurate determination of imatinib is essential. Present analytical methods to detect imatinib mainly contain high performance liquid chromatography–UV detector (HPLC–UV) [20], liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS) [21], liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS–MS) [22], capillary electrophoresis [23] and electrochemical analysis [24,25]. However, the chromatography techniques with high selectivity and sensitivity require expensive equipment and toxic solvents. Conversely, the fluorescence method for the determination of imatinib based on nanoprobes, which is quick, highly sensitive and requires relatively inexpensive equipment, is rarely reported.

In our study, we proposed a two-step sustainable synthetic route to prepare CDs without further surface modification. The starch is employed as carbon source, and CDs is obtained through soft chemical oxidation with mixed oxidant (
V
HAc
:
V
H 2
O 2
= 1 : 2
). Based on fluorescence quenching, a novel method for the determination of imatinib was developed with as-prepared CDs.

Starch, potassium periodate (KIO4), hydrochloric acid (36–38%) and acetic acid (HAc) were all purchased from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Corporation (Nanjing, China). 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Limited Corporation (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or the best grade commercially available. Doubly distilled water (DDW) was used throughout the study.

Fluorescence spectra and intensities were performed on a RF-5301PC spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-2100 UV/Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were conducted on a FTIR-8400S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). TEM images were acquired by using an H-7650 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 80kV. All pH value measurements were obtained by a pHS-25 pH (Leici, shanghai). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions.

Following a method described previously [26], the black carbon source was obtained by high temperature and pressure treatment of the starch. As-obtained carbon source and mixed oxidant (
V
HAc
:
V
H 2
O 2
= 1 : 2
) were refluxed for 8h at 120°C. Bright yellow CDs solution was subjected to 2000 NWCO of dialysis membrane for 24h. Finally, light yellow CDs powder was obtained by vacuum drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of imatinib with as-prepared CDs
ce:section.ce:para: 2.0mL of CDs solution (1.038mgmL−1) was transferred into 50mL brown volumetric flask, and then diluted with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 3.0). 10μL of imatinib solution (1.010mgmL−1) was added into 1.0mL of CDs solution, and thoroughly mixed. Keeping at room temperature for 5min, the fluorescence intensity (F, CDs solution after adding imatinib) was observed when excited at 350nm. At the same time the fluorescence intensity of blank test (F 0, CDs solution without adding imatinib) was measured.

The well-dispersed CDs solution exhibits bright blue fluorescence under ultraviolet lamp, which could be easily observed with the naked eye (inset in Fig. 2A). The as-obtained CDs exhibit some property of excitation-independent emissions under varied excitation wavelengths, and the emission peaks had slightly blue shift. The emission band with highest-level fluorescence intensity was observed around 450nm when excited at 350nm (Fig. 2A).

FT-IR spectra were recorded to identify the surface groups of as-prepared CDs (Fig. 2B). The broad peak centered at 3435cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH. The sharp peaks at 1714 and 1234cm−1 are the characteristic absorption band of CO and CO, respectively. Several peaks at 2983, 2973, 1620 and 1393cm−1 reveal the existence of CC, CH2 and CH3 on the surface of CDs [27,28]. Thus, these confirm that as-obtained CDs are applied in subsequent quantitative analysis.

This method is based on fluorescence quenching to determine imatinib. In order to obtain a wide linear range and high sensitivity, keeping high fluorescence intensity (F 0) and fluorescence quenching rate ((F 0 − F)/F 0) of CDs are necessary.

The CDs concentration influences the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quenching rate of the detection system. The system was studied in CDs concentration range from 10.13 to 60.75μgmL−1. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity (F 0) increased gradually with increasing CDs concentration; the fluorescence quenching rate ((F 0 − F)/F 0) is the highest at 41.50μgmL−1 CDs solution. Therefore, 41.50μgmL−1 of CDs solution was chosen in this study.

There are some difference in terms of the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quenching rate under different pH of the system. Therefore, we investigated the effect of pH form 1.00 to 11.0 in PBS buffer system. As shown in Fig. 3B, (F 0 − F)/F 0 increased with increasing pH from 1.00 to 3.00. After that, (F 0 − F)/F 0 decreased gradually. Thus, a pH value of 3.00 was chosen in this study.

The differences of fluorescence intensity after adding imatinib under different temperature were also studied. As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity (F) change little at the temperature from 15 to 30°C; when the reaction temperature is higher than 30°C, the fluorescence intensity (F) slowly enhances with the increase of the temperature of the system, and (F 0 − F)/F 0 is reduced. Therefore, this method can be used at room temperature.

In addition, the effect of reaction time between CDs and imatinib were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity (F) was almost no change within an hour. Thus, the fluorescence intensity (F) was recorded when CDs solution was added imatinib after 5min.

Under optimum conditions, different concentrations of imatinib were added into CDs solution (41.50μgmL−1, pH 3.0 of PBS buffer). Keeping 5min at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity (F 0 and F) of the system was measured with an excitation wavelength of 350nm. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CDs is sensitive to imatinib and decreases with increasing imatinib concentration. There is a good semilogarithmic correlation between the quenching efficiency (F 0/F) and the concentration of imatinib in the range from 10 to 400μgmL−1 (see the inset of Fig. 5 ) via the following equation:
F 0
/ F − 1 = 0.0016 C + 0.0086
(
R 2
= 0.9977 )
where F 0 and F are the FL intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of imatinib and C represents the concentration of imatinib. The detection limit of imatinib (C L =3δ/k, where C L is the detection limit, δ is the standard deviation of the solvent blank and k is the slope of the linear regression equation [29]) was calculated to be 2.4×10−3 μgmL−1. These results show that the as-obtained CDs with strong FL activity have very promising application in the detection of imatinib.

To evaluate the extraction procedures and the accuracy of the method, a recovery assay was performed. The recoveries at different concentration sample solutions were satisfactory (as shown in Table 1 ).

Moreover, the interference of foreign substances in the determination of imatinib was examined. As shown in Table 2 , the results show that the coexisting substances are tolerated in relatively high concentration, and had no interference on the determination of imatinib.

Currently, hypothesis of fluorescence quenching mechanism are mainly the following aspects: energy transfer, inner filter effect, charge transfer and the formation of polymers [30–33]. To obtain the possible mechanisms, the following experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 5A, UV absorption spectrum of imatinib (curve a) and the fluorescent spectrum of CDs (curve b) have no significant overlap part, explains that the detection system does not occur fluorescence resonance energy transfer. As shown in Fig. 5B, UV absorption spectrum of a mixed solution of CDs and imatinib produced no new peaks, and were basically the same as the superposition data of them (curve d). It shows the absorption spectrum of the CDs detection system did not change before and after the addition of imatinib, namely there were not the formation of polymers between CDs and imatinib.

To further study the mechanism, the next experiments were conducted. The FT-IR spectra of CDs and CDs-imatinib are shown in Fig. 5C. The sharp peaks at 3410 and 3336cm−1 are the characteristic absorption band of NH (imatinib: curve b). However, FT-IR spectra of CDs-imatinib (curve a) show that the broad peak centered at 3435cm−1 only corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH. It would be observed that FT-IR spectra have been some changes after adding imatinib in CDs system. The characteristic peak of NH disappeared probably due to amino group protonated, but also the carboxyl groups of CDs may generate an electrostatic attraction action with imatinib. Thus, it may be caused by electrostatic electron density change, namely the charge transfer caused fluorescence quenching (as shown in Fig. 5D).

In summary, we have presented a simple method for the synthesis of water-soluble CDs by using the starch as the precursor. It would be concluded that the as-prepared CDs exhibit high fluorescence intensity, excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior and bright blue fluorescence under UV light irradiation. Based on the selective quenching phenomenon, a novel fluorescence method for the determination of imatinib was developed with as-synthesized CDs. This method is confirmed with good linearity, low detection limit and satisfactory recovery. The possible quenching mechanism between CDs and imatinib was found to be charge transfer. This work is important to widen the investigation and application of fluorescent CDs for drug detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the following foundations and projects: The Research and Innovation Project for Graduate Students Academic Degree of Colleges and Universities of Jiangsu Province (KYZZ_0185).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Zhengyu Yan is a professor of pharmaceutical analysis at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
Mr. Zhengwei Zhang is a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on drug spectral analysis.
Dr. Jianqiu Chen is an assistant professor of pharmaceutical analysis at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
